evidence: foundational investments in technology and artificial intelligence to position ourselves to be fit for the future, building a set of high impact commercial, operational, and digital capabilities

investments in technology and digitalization, including artificial intelligence and data analytics to enhance our consumer insights and research

This allows us to unify data, gain real-time inventory visibility, and provide faster, more responsive customer service
Through our collaborations with cutting-edge technology providers, we are using artificial intelligence to reimagine our go-to-market model

rapid evolution and increased adoption of artificial intelligence technologies may increase our cybersecurity risks, including generative artificial intelligence augmenting threat actors technological sophistication to enhance existing or create new malware
our use of artificial intelligence may result in increased claims of infringement or other claims, including those based on unauthorized use of third-party technology or content

increasing adoption of artificial intelligence technologies has led data protection authorities in many jurisdictions to consider and adopt new and evolving interpretations of privacy and data protection laws

limitations: No AI-attributable dollar amounts or quantified investment figures found in excerpts; investment intensity scored 3 (named programs, no quantified scale).
The $6.15 billion productivity plan mentions technology/automation but does not explicitly attribute any portion to AI; excluded from capex_mentions per anti-inflation rule.

Narrative concreteness scored 4 based on described deployments (go-to-market, inventory visibility, customer service) but no quantified business results (%, $, volume) were disclosed; promotional language noted.

Risk disclosure scored 4 based on named company-specific risk mechanisms (cybersecurity, IP infringement, data privacy regulation) with described impact pathways, but no quantification or named governance bodies found in excerpts.
